skill.name: docker-helper
skill.description: Docker Compose generation, optimization, and troubleshooting assistance.
skill.summary: Docker Compose generation, optimization, and troubleshooting assistance.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly false positives from Markdown examples for Dockerfiles, Compose files, and shell commands. The skill has no executable helper code and no evidence of prompt injection or data exfiltration, but it includes Docker cleanup commands and plaintext credential examples that warrant a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Docker Cleanup Commands
medium_findings.description: The command reference includes Docker prune commands that can remove unused images, containers, networks, build cache, and volumes. This is legitimate Docker maintenance guidance, but users could lose local development data if they run it without understanding the impact.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly shown in a cleanup section and can delete Docker resources. They are not malicious because they are documented examples, but they are genuinely hazardous if copied blindly.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext Credential Examples
medium_findings.description: The Compose examples include database URLs, Redis URLs, an env_file reference, and an example database password. These are illustrative configuration snippets, but marketplace users may copy them into real projects without replacing secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are examples inside Compose documentation, not secret exfiltration code. Risk remains because plaintext placeholder credentials are easy to reuse accidentally.

low_findings.title: Dockerfile Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command findings come from fenced Dockerfile snippets showing RUN, CMD, ENTRYPOINT, and HEALTHCHECK patterns. The skill does not include executable scripts that automatically run these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected commands are inside Markdown code fences and are normal Docker guidance. No file contains a script, wrapper, or automation path that executes them.

low_findings.title: Recursive Delete Finding Is Scoped Docker Image Cleanup
low_findings.description: The recursive delete pattern removes apt package-list cache inside a Dockerfile layer. It targets /var/lib/apt/lists/*, not root or a user home directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a standard Docker image size reduction pattern following apt-get clean. The path is narrowly scoped to package metadata.

low_findings.title: Network URLs Are Local or Service-Internal Examples
low_findings.description: The URL detections are Compose service names or localhost health checks. No evidence found of external endpoints, credential transfer, or unauthorized network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to Docker service names or localhost health endpoints. They do not reference public collection services or remote hosts.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Troubleshooting Commands
low_findings.description: The docker run, inspect, and exec examples are normal container debugging commands. They inspect user-selected containers and do not perform covert host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are labeled as troubleshooting steps for containers. They require explicit user execution and do not enumerate host secrets or system identity.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Has No Supporting Code
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography location is the YAML description line. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or cryptographic algorithm use in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata only. The reviewed file contains no crypto APIs or algorithm names at that location.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static heuristic combined command, network, and credential patterns, but the file is a single Markdown guide. No evidence found of obfuscation, automation, prompt injection, or credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The concerning patterns appear in separate instructional snippets. There is no executable code path linking credentials, network calls, and command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Pastable Destructive Docker Maintenance Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: docker system prune -a and docker volume prune are useful maintenance commands, but they can remove data outside the current project. Users should review Docker resources before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and destructive by design. The risk is user-error data loss, not malicious execution by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Pastable Placeholder Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: The Compose examples show placeholder database credentials and environment file usage. Users should replace them with secret management before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are visible and can be copied directly. The skill also advises secrets management, which lowers confidence that this is an intentional unsafe pattern.

content.value_statement: Docker configuration is easy to make slow, insecure, or hard to debug. This skill helps generate, review, and troubleshoot Dockerfiles and Compose setups.
actual_capabilities: Generate Dockerfile examples for Node.js, Python, and Go applications.
Create multi-service Docker Compose examples with networks, volumes, and health checks.
Suggest image-size optimizations, including multi-stage builds and layer caching.
Troubleshoot common container startup, networking, volume, and build issues.
Recommend Docker security practices such as non-root users and version-pinned images.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, but it does not inspect running containers directly.
Generated commands may affect local Docker resources if users run them manually.
Example credentials and URLs must be replaced before production use.
It does not replace a full container security review or vulnerability scanner.

use_cases.title: Create a Development Compose Stack
use_cases.description: Generate a local Compose setup with an app service, database, volumes, and live reload conventions.

use_cases.title: Optimize a Slow Docker Build
use_cases.description: Review a Dockerfile and suggest layer caching, dependency installation, and multi-stage build improvements.

use_cases.title: Debug a Container Startup Failure
use_cases.description: Walk through logs, entrypoint behavior, networking, and health checks to identify likely failure causes.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic Dockerfile
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single service
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Dockerfile for my application. Ask for the language, package manager, exposed port, and production start command before writing it.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Compose File
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Docker Compose setup for my web app, API, database, and cache. Include networks, volumes, health checks, and clear placeholders for secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize an Existing Dockerfile
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Dockerfile for build speed, cache efficiency, final image size, and security. Explain each recommended change before applying it.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Production Containers
prompt_templates.prompt: Help diagnose this container failure using logs, Compose configuration, health checks, networking, volumes, and resource limits. Prioritize safe diagnostic commands.

output_examples.input: Generate Docker support for a Node.js API with PostgreSQL and Redis.
output: A production-oriented Dockerfile plan using a build stage and a non-root runtime user.
A Compose design with API, database, cache, private network, persistent volumes, and health checks.
Notes explaining which placeholders must be replaced with secret management.

output_examples.input: My container exits immediately after starting.
output: A diagnostic checklist covering logs, command and entrypoint settings, missing environment variables, and failed health checks.
A short list of safe commands to inspect the selected container.
Likely fixes ranked from most common to least common.

output_examples.input: Review this Dockerfile for image size and security.
output: Specific recommendations for dependency caching, multi-stage builds, pinned base images, and non-root execution.
Warnings about secrets, broad file copies, and unnecessary packages.
A concise before-and-after explanation of expected improvements.

best_practices: Use pinned base image versions and rebuild regularly for security updates.
Keep secrets outside Dockerfiles and replace sample credentials before deployment.
Review Docker cleanup commands before running them on a shared workstation or CI host.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder passwords or database URLs into production files.
Do not run prune commands unless you understand which Docker data can be removed.
Do not use broad COPY instructions without a maintained .dockerignore file.

faq.question: Can this skill run Docker commands for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide whether to run any command.

faq.question: Does it support Docker Compose?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft and review Compose files with services, networks, volumes, and health checks.

faq.question: Can it make production-ready Dockerfiles?
faq.answer: It can recommend production patterns, but teams should still review security, secrets, and deployment requirements.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes copy-pastable Docker cleanup commands and placeholder credentials. They are useful but require user care.

faq.question: Does it include malicious code?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed file is a Markdown guide with examples, not executable helper code.

faq.question: Which AI coding tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
